Abstract
Bimanual dexterous grasping is a fundamental and promising area in robotics, yet its progress is constrained by the lack of comprehensive datasets and powerful generation models. In this work, we propose BiDexGrasp, consists of a large-scale bimanual dexterous grasp dataset and a novel generation model. For dataset, we propose a novel bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline to efficiently annotate physically feasible data for dataset construction. This pipeline addresses the challenges of high-dimensional bimanual grasping through a two-stage synthesis strategy of efficient region-based grasp initialization and decoupled force-closure grasp optimization. Powered by this pipeline, we construct a large-scale bimanual dexterous grasp dataset, comprising 6351 diverse objects with sizes ranging from 30 to 80 cm, along with 9.7 million annotated grasp data. Based on this dataset, we further introduce a bimanual-coordinated and geometry-size-adaptive dexterous grasping generation framework. The framework lies in two key designs: a bimanual coordination module and a geometry-size-adaptive grasp generation strategy to generate coordinated and high-quality grasps on unseen objects. Extensive experiments conducted in both simulation and real world demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed data synthesis pipeline and learned generative framework.

Currently, several recent studies have explored data synthesis for bimanual dexterous grasping
[
26
,
17
]
, primarily by extending single-hand grasp pipelines to the bimanual setting. However, these dataset still focus on objects of limited size ranges and fail to fully leverage the advantages of bimanual manipulation for handling objects with diverse scales. Meanwhile, we observe that both directly transferring state-of-the-art single-hand methods or applying existing bimanual methods perform poorly on objects with diverse geometries and scales. We attribute this to the dual challenges of varied object sizes and enlarged bimanual search spaces: the number of initialization candidates grows quadratically, and the subsequent force-closure optimization occurs in a high-dimensional space, significantly reducing both efficiency and grasp quality.
In this work, we introduce BiDexGrasp for coordinated bimanual dexterous grasping of objects with diverse sizes and geometries. First, we construct a large-scale, high-quality bimanual dexterous grasp dataset using a novel and effective two-stage data synthesis pipeline. Second, we propose a bimanual-coordinated and geometry-size-adaptive grasp generation framework that learns from this dataset, capable of producing high-quality grasp poses for diverse unseen objects.
For the dataset construction, we propose a novel bimanual dexterous grasp synthesis pipeline for effective and high-quality dataset construction.
The motivation is to tackle the challenges: the larger action space leads to increased complexity and reduced efficiency. To improve the efficiency, we introduce an efficient bimanual region-constraint grasp initialization strategy to obtain coordinated bi-grasp initializations with physically feasible potential. To improve the quality, we propose a decoupled force-closure grasp optimization strategy, which separates the force-closure of each hand. This decoupling not only reduces the overall optimization complexity, but also ensures that both hands achieve high-quality local grasps. Compared with previous bimanual grasp synthesis pipelines, our method achieves more than 2.8x the success rate and over 30Г— faster synthesis speed. Using this pipeline, we construct a large-scale and high-quality bimanual dexterous grasp dataset containing 6,351 objects with diverse geometries and scales and 9.7 million grasps, as shown in Tab.
I
.
Based on the dataset, we propose data-driven model, BiDexGrasp framework, for high-quality grasp generation on diverse unseen objects. The key motivation of this framework is how to achieve bimanual coordination and how to generalize across diverse geometries and sizes. To achieve coordination, we introduce a bimanual coordination module to predict coordinated grasp views, to guide the feature extraction and grasp poses generation. To achieve adaptation, we propose a geometry-size-adaptive grasp strategy that generates relative grasp poses conditioned on a size-adaptive grasp anchor and geometry-aware object features. This design reduces the action space and encourages the model to focus on local structures, thereby improving robustness to geometric and scale variations.
Extensive simulation and real-world experiments validate our data synthesis pipeline and learned grasp generation framework. The data collection experiments show high quality and good efficiency of our synthesis pipeline. And the model generation experiments in on simulation dataset and physical robots show good performance of our framework.
II
Related work
II-A
Bimanul Dexterous Grasp
Bimanual dexterous grasping is a promising research direction that extends grasping to large and heavy objects, yet remains underexplored due to the high dimension of the combined degrees of freedom (DOF). Some previous works
[
39
,
3
]
rely on reinforcement learning, which face challenges in scalability and depends on well designed reward functions. Other methods
[
26
,
17
]
adopt data-driven frameworks by extending single-hand dexterous grasping models to the bimanual setting. However, these approaches are often limited by the availability of high-quality datasets and suboptimal generative architectures. In this work, we address these challenges by constructing a large-scale dataset with high diversity, quality, and practical relevance, and proposing a novel generative framework tailored for bimanual dexterous grasping.

To overcome these, we introduce a novel pipeline for bimanual dexterous grasp synthesis and present a large-scale dataset that prioritizes diversity, quality, and real-world applicability.

Object Preparation
. For the preparation of objects with sufficient shape and scale diversity, we collect 3D objects from the Objaverse
[

[

This results in a 6,351 object final set of 2,397 objects from DexGraspNet and 3,954 objects from Objaverse. To expand the scale variation of the experimental objects, each individual object is scaled to 11 discrete sizes, with the size range set from 30 cm to 80 cm.
Synthesis Pipeline Overview
.
The pipeline consists of three stages: initialization, optimization, and filtering.
First, we employ a fast bimanual constraintвҖ“aware grasp initialization strategy to generate grasp candidates with the potential to satisfy physical feasibility of bimanual grasping.
Next, a decoupled force-closureвҖ“based optimization is performed to optimize the initial candidates into physically valid bimanual grasps.
Finally, we provide an arm-reachability analysis to derive kinematically feasible configurations.
The synthesis pipeline is built upon the advanced framework proposed in
[
2
]
, which is implemented on top of cuRobo
[
29
]
to enable efficient GPU-parallelized grasp synthesis.
IV-B
Bimanual region-constraint Grasp Initialization
Challenges for Bimanual Grasp Initialization
.
Extending single grasp initialization, which samples uniformly within the objectвҖҷs convex hull, results in quadratic growth in sampling complexity.
We find that most of the random sampled pairs fail to satisfy the bimanual kinematic and coordination constraints of the bimanual system.
To address these, we propose a bimanual region-constraint grasp initialization strategy. First, grasping region candidates are efficiently selected based on the grasp wrench space (GWS).
Second, initial grasp poses are sampled from the selected regions, enabling both efficient and reliable initialization for subsequent optimization.

for subsequent optimization. Each hand pose is represented by the palm position, orientation, and an in-plane rotation about the palm normal. The palm position is chosen as the point on the dilated object convex hull closest to the grasping region, with the opposite surface normal defining the orientation. The in-plane rotation is determined by a predefined ideal direction for each hand. Finger joint angles are initialized based on local surface curvature, starting from a fully open posture and slightly closing to conform to the object surface.
Bimanual Feasibility Verification.
As a final step, we verify the physical and kinematic feasibility of all initialized bimanual grasp configurations.
We first perform collision checking to prevent self-collisions between the two hands as well as collisions with the object and the environment.
We then solve inverse kinematics (IK) for both arms to evaluate collision-free reachability.
Grasp initializations that fail the above feasibility checks are discarded.
IV-C
Decoupled Grasping Optimization
Challenges for Bimanual Grasp Optimization.
Bimanual grasp optimization is inherently challenging due to the increased complexity of the optimization space, leading to a tightly coupled objective that hinders stable and efficient optimization.
And such coupled objectives may yield unbalanced solutions, in which one hand dominates grasp stability while the other contributes marginally, causing the optimization to converge to suboptimal local minima.
Figure 3:
BiDexGrasp Framework. Given the input object point cloud and pre-defined grasp view, the framework first predicts a pair of coordinated grasp views for the two hands by bimanual coordination module. Based on this view, we extract geometry-adaptive object feature and determine the scale-adaptive grasp. Then the relative dexterous grasp diffusion is employed to predict relative dexterous grasp form the grasp anchor.
Decoupled Force Closure
To obtain high-quality and physically feasible bimanual grasps, we propose a decoupled optimization strategy based on grasping energy.
The key idea is to decompose the bimanual grasp optimization into single-hand energy terms, allowing each hand to focus on establishing stable local contacts with the object.
This decoupling not only reduces the optimization complexity, but also discourages imbalanced solutions dominated by a single hand, resulting in improved bimanual grasp quality and convergence stability.

BiDexGrasp Framework
Problem definition.
To learn the generable bi-manual dexterous grasp, we design a data-driven generation model.
Specifically, given the object point cloud
рқ’Ә

Challenge in bimanual grasping.
Bimanual grasp generation introduces two key challenges.
(1)
Bimanual coordination
: Bimanual hands must collaboratively target optimal grasp view to ensure global stability while avoiding inter-hand collisions.
(2)
Adaptation across object geometries and sizes
: The vast diversity in object sizes and geometries greatly hinders efficient model learning, increasing the difficulty of object feature extraction and expanding the action space simultaneously.
Model overview.
To address these challenges, we propose BiDexGrasp as shown in Fig.
5
, to achieve bimanual-coordinated and geometry-size-adaptive grasp generation:
(1)
bimanual coordination module
that explicitly models bimanual hand relationships via coordinated-view prediction;
(2)
geometry-size-adaptive grasp generation strategy
to improve the generation ability across object geometries and sizes by adaptive grasp anchors. Specifically, the bimanual coordination module first predicts coordinated grasp views. Based on these views, the model then determines the adaptive grasp base on the objectвҖҷs bounding sphere and generates relative dexterous poses from the grasp anchor.

The adaptive grasp anchor is determine dynamically according to the grasp view and object point cloud. Specifically, each predicted view intersects with minimum bounding sphere surface, producing an grasp anchor. This anchor-based formulation converts the original absolute pose prediction problem into a relative regression task, significantly reducing the search space of grasps and improve the adaptation to object scale.

Speed (S)
and
Success Generation Speed (SGS)
(grasps/s) measures the number of raw grasps and successful grasps synthesized per second, which is evaluated on a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU, while the baseline speeds are cited from their original reports.
Penetration Depth (PD)
(unit: cm) measures the maximum intersection distance between the object and the hand.
Self-Penetration Depth (SPD)
(unit: cm) is defined as the maximum penetration distance among all hand collision meshes, including both intra- and inter-hand self-penetrations, while ignoring collisions between neighboring links.
Contact Distance Consistency (CDC)
(unit: cm) measures the range between the maximum and minimum signed contact distances across all fingers, reflecting the uniformity of fingerвҖ“object contacts and being invariant to penetration.
First Variance Ratio (D)
(%) is the explained variance ratio of the first principal component from PCA, quantifying the concentration of bimanual grasp distributions.

Data synthesis experiments
Comparison results.
We compare our approach with BimanGrasp, the only publicly available open-source bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline. As BimanGrasp is restricted to floating-base grasp generation, we evaluate our method in both floating and tabletop settings to ensure a fair comparison while demonstrating practical feasibility.
Tab.
II
shows that our bimanual data synthesis pipeline significantly outperforms prior work in both grasp success rate (SUC) and synthesis speed (
S
S
).
On DexGraspNet, our method increases the success rate by roughly 2.8Г— under both floating-hand and tabletop settings, regardless of whether robot arms are considered, while achieving a 40Г— speedup in synthesis.
On Objaverse, which features more complex object geometries, our method similarly demonstrates strong grasp success rates alongside high synthesis efficiency.
Figure 6:
The visualization of dexterous grasping generated by our framework in simulation dataset.
Ablation Study.
Tab.
III
shows the ablation study for each component of our synthesis pipeline. The baseline is a straightforward extension of BoDex
[
2
]
from the single-hand setting to a bimanual setup. The results show that R (Region initialization), Dc (decoupled force-closure) and Ps (pre-grasp strategies) all contribute to improving the grasp success rate (SUC-L and SUC-A) and synthesis speed (SGS).
VI-E
Generation framework experiments
Comparison results.
Tab.
IV
reports the performance of our method against recent state-of-the-art approaches. Our approach achieves the highest grasp success rate (SUC) while maintaining the lowest object penetration (PD) and self-penetration (SPD), demonstrating precise and stable grasps. Although the grasp diversity (D) is slightly lower, this is a trade-off can be accepted since our task primarily emphasizes grasp success. Overall, our method consistently outperforms existing methods in generating physically feasible and reliable grasps, and the visualization in Fig.
6
also supports it.
Ablation study.
Tab.
V
shows the effectiveness of each component in our framework. The baseline (first row) is a straightforward DDPM-based bimanual grasp generation baseline. Incorporating the Bimanual Coordination Module (BCM) significantly improves grasp success rate, highlighting its importance for coordinated dual-hand grasps. The Geometry-Adaptive Feature (GAF) enhances adaptation to local object geometry, while the Scale-Adaptive Grasp Anchor (SAGA) improves adaptation to object scale, together boosting SUC and stabilizing grasps (lower SPD and CDC). The full configuration achieves the best performance, demonstrating the complementary contributions of each component.
VI-F
Efficiency analysis of Framework
To further evaluate our generation framework, we conduct ablation studies on inference time. We measure the inference time of a single batch with batch size 64 and compare our method against the DDPM-Baseline (removes the BCM, GAF and SAGA component). To reduce measurement variance, we run each experiment for five epochs and report the mean and standard deviation. As shown in Table

, and the results indicate that our method significantly improves the success rate at the cost of only a modest increase in inference time.

VI-G
Visualization of other dexterous hands
Our data synthesis pipeline is not limited to a specific hand model and can be naturally extended to other dexterous hands. Fig.
8
visualizes grasp samples generated by applying our pipeline to the Leap Hand

The visualization of failure cases in the generation results of our framework. We find that the common failure cases are caused by object-hand penetration and non-contact.
Figure 8:
The visualization of the LeapHand grasp poses synthesized by our data synthesis pipeline, showing that our synthesis pipeline can be used for different dexterous hands.
VI-H
Failure Case Analysis
As illustrated in Fig.
7
, failure cases primarily manifest as physical instability. Minor object penetration or floating grasps may occur when the generative model fails to perfectly balance the penetration penalty and contact supervision. Furthermore, as demonstrated in the rightmost case, a small number of objects undergo lateral sliding due to insufficient force closure, resulting in grasp failure.
VI-I
Real-World Experiments
In real world experiments, the robot hardware is set in two different hands, Briainco hand with Piper arm and Inspire hand with Untree G1 robots as shown in Figure
9
. We use a Realsense depth camera to capture RGB-D observations. Then the object mesh is reconstructed using Hyper3D
[
14
]
, with its scale determined by aligning the bounding box of the partial object point clouds. The reconstructed mesh is then sampled to obtain the object point cloud, which is fed into our generation framework to produce Shadow Hand poses. These poses are subsequently retargeted to the Briainco or Inspire hand using a hand-object consistent retargeting scheme. The robotic arms reach the dexterous wrists 6-DoF poses via motion planning. Further implementation details are provided in the supplementary material.
The experiment results in conducted on unseen real-world objects of various sizes and geometries, performing six grasp attempts for each object under different dexterous hands.
The experimental results in Table
VII
show that, by learning from the proposed dataset, our framework can generate high-quality bimanual dexterous grasps.
Figure 9:
The visualization of real world hardware platform and the objects used in experiments.
VII
Conclusion
In this work, we propose BiDexGrasp, a comprehensive solution to advance bimanual dexterous grasping through both large-scale dataset and learning-based framework. The proposed bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline can effectively produces physically feasible and high-quality grasp annotations via region-based initialization and decoupled force-closure optimization. Using this pipeline, we build a large-scale dataset containing 6,351 diverse objects and 9.7 million annotated bimanual grasps.
Based upon this dataset, we proposed a bimanual-coordinated and geometry-size-adaptive grasp generation framework that explicitly models inter-hand coordination and improves adaptability to object geometry and scale. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world settings demonstrate that our data synthesis pipeline significantly improves efficiency and quality, while our generation framework achieves coordinated and high-success-rate bimanual dexterous grasps across diverse objects.
We hope BiDexGrasp will promote future research on scalable, generalizable, and physically grounded bimanual dexterous grasping and manipulation.

preview_text:
Bimanual dexterous grasping is a fundamental and promising area in robotics, yet its progress is constrained by the lack of comprehensive datasets and powerful generation models. In this work, we propose BiDexGrasp, consists of a large-scale bimanual dexterous grasp dataset and a novel generation model. For dataset, we propose a novel bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline to efficiently annotate physically feasible data for dataset construction. This pipeline addresses the challenges of high-dimensional bimanual grasping through a two-stage synthesis strategy of efficient region-based grasp initialization and decoupled force-closure grasp optimization. Powered by this pipeline, we construct a large-scale bimanual dexterous grasp dataset, comprising 6351 diverse objects with sizes ranging from 30 to 80 cm, along with 9.7 million annotated grasp data. Based on this dataset, we further introduce a bimanual-coordinated and geometry-size-adaptive dexterous grasping generation framework. The framework lies in two key designs: a bimanual coordination module and a geometry-size-adaptive grasp generation strategy to generate coordinated and high-quality grasps on unseen objects. Extensive experiments conducted in both simulation and real world demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed data synthesis pipeline and learned generative framework.
